scenes.title: The scheduler heap
scenes.tagline: Kubernetes PR #139003 — map lookups out of the hot path
scenes.footnote: +541 / −133   ·   2 files   ·   a performance refactor, reviewed honestly

beats.narration: A pull request against Kubernetes. Pull request one-three-nine-thousand-and-three.
It is a performance refactor of one core data structure -- the priority heap the scheduler uses to pick the next pod. No new feature, no API change. Just: faster.
Two files, five hundred lines. We will not just admire it -- we will review it: the design, the trade it makes, the tests, and a verdict.

scenes.heading: A heap on the critical path

nodes.label: Less / Swap
nodes.sub: the O(log n) comparison hot path

beats.narration: The Kubernetes scheduler keeps pending pods in priority heaps, so it can always pick the highest-priority pod next. Three different pending-pod queues all build on the same heap.
Underneath all three is one generic heap implementation -- a single file: backend, heap, heap dot go.
And inside the heap, two functions sit squarely on the critical path -- Less and Swap. They are called order-log-n times for every Push, every Pop, every Fix.

In a large cluster with thousands of pending pods, the cost of those two tiny functions multiplies across thousands of operations every second. That is the cost this pull request goes after -- and the question a review must answer is what it pays to get there.

scenes.heading: Keys and a map, became pointers
scenes.code:
// The heap's backing storage.
 type data[T any] struct {
-    // Before: a slice of KEYS, plus a side map from key to item.
-    // Every comparison pays for two hash-map lookups.
-    items map[string]*heapItem[T]
-    queue []string
+    // After: the queue holds item POINTERS, directly.
+    // keyIndex is consulted only for key-based lookups.
+    queue    []*heapItem[T]
+    keyIndex map[string]int
 }

beats.narration: Here is the redesign. Before, the heap stored a slice of string keys -- plus a side map, from each key to its item.
The consequence: every comparison between two heap positions performed two hash-map lookups, purely to find the objects it was meant to compare.
After, the queue holds the item pointers directly. The heap order lives in the slice itself -- there is no indirection left to resolve.

The rejected alternative is simply to keep the old shape: queue as strings, items in a map, and keep paying two hash-map lookups every time Less or Swap runs. This pull request chooses the pointer queue instead because the scheduler pays that cost order-log-n times per Push, Pop, and Fix, at benchmark sizes of one hundred, one thousand, and five thousand items, and the measured return is twenty-four to forty-six percent faster operations with roughly half the allocations. The price is new bookkeeping: every mutating path now has to keep queue and keyIndex in lockstep, and Pop has to clear the freed slot.

scenes.heading: Seven lines became one

beats.narration: And here is the payoff, in the comparison function itself. Before, Less was seven lines -- a bounds check, two map lookups, two not-found guards, and finally the compare.
Every one of those lookups is a hash, a probe, a pointer-chase -- paid on every single comparison, order-log-n times per operation.
After, Less is one line. A direct dereference into the slice, and the compare. The hash-map lookups are simply gone.

But notice what else left: the bounds guards. On the real Kubernetes path, that is dead code removal, because container-slash-heap is the caller and it does not pass Less or Swap an out-of-range index. The cost is narrower than a behavior change but still real: the heap keeps less defense-in-depth against internal misuse, because a bad index now panics instead of quietly returning false.

scenes.heading: Faster is proven. Correct is the question.

beats.narration: Does it pay off? The author benchmarked it at heap sizes of one hundred, one thousand, and five thousand pods, and rewrote the heap tests in the same pull request.

The speedup is real. Pop drops from sixteen-point-seven milliseconds to nine-point-six, AddOrUpdate and Fix from two-point-seven to one-point-five, Mixed from fifteen-point-one to eight-point-two. Push and Mixed allocations are roughly halved, which matters on a garbage-collected hot path.

Now the correctness evidence. TestHeapPopOrder proves the ordering claim directly. TestHeapDeleteMiddle and TestHeapUpdatePriority exercise the mutation paths most likely to go wrong. TestHeapLargeScale adds broader churn, which matters because the safety net was rebuilt in the same pull request as the refactor.

That is stronger than saying 'there are tests,' but weaker than saying the tests prove everything. The rewritten assertions do pin the new representation directly -- queue zero dot key and keyIndex for baz -- and they lock in pop order, delete and update behavior, and large-scale churn. Separately, the code now nils the freed slot before truncation to avoid a retained-pointer leak. What the tests do not prove is the removed-guard trust boundary; that part is a caller-contract judgment about container-slash-heap staying the only source of indices into Less and Swap, while the G C E one-hundred performance run only says the refactor survived real scheduler load.

nodes.label: Approve
nodes.sub: the win outweighs the new invariant burden

nodes.label: Risk — stale keyIndex
nodes.sub: high churn can misroute later mutations

nodes.label: Why approve
nodes.sub: callers untouched; tests pin heap behavior

beats.narration: So -- the verdict. And a verdict is not a compliment. Approve: this is a clean, genuinely well-measured refactor of a real hot path, with no public A-P-I change. But here is what I would still say in the review.

The narrower weird-input case is the removed guardrail. Less and Swap no longer try to return false on bad indices; they trust container-slash-heap to be the only caller, and the production path does exactly that. So this part of the change is justified by caller contract, not by the rewritten unit tests: if a future internal caller violates it, the failure mode is immediate panic.

The higher-churn failure mode is the new invariant itself. If any mutating path updates queue without updating keyIndex, the heap does not just get slower. The next AddOrUpdate, Delete, or GetByKey can hit the wrong slot or an out-of-range index, so under churn correctness breaks first and a panic can follow. By contrast, the retained-tail-pointer path would only degrade memory and G C pressure, and this pull request explicitly closes that path by nil-ing the freed slot on Pop. TestHeapLargeScale is the right unit test for the first class of bug; the G C E one-hundred performance run is not, because it measures throughput under load, not proof that a rare desync can never happen.

Why still approve? Because the blast radius stayed tight. activeQueue, backoffQueue, and schedulingQueue needed no edits, the public Heap A-P-I is unchanged, and the rewritten tests directly pin pop order, delete-middle, update-priority, and large-scale churn against the new representation.

So the ruling is approve, with one watch item. The wins are large enough to justify the new bookkeeping: twenty-four to forty-six percent faster heap operations, roughly half the allocations on Push and Mixed, and a pass on the kubernetes E two E G C E one-hundred performance job. The thing to watch in future reviews is not public version skew; it is whether every mutator keeps queue and keyIndex in lockstep, because that invariant now carries the full correctness burden.
